name: Corneja Valley Trail
name_original: La Aldehuela, Carrascalejo, Hoyorredondo y puente romano de La Fonseca
description: This moderate 17.3-mile mountain biking route combines diverse terrains and scenery, including the historic Ponseca Bridge.
region_intro: Mountain bike trails La Horcajada traverse a diverse landscape, characterized by valleys, hills, and the foothills of the Sierra de Gredos. The terrain offers varied elevation gains, with some routes climbing over 1,000 feet. This region provides a mix of natural features, including river valleys and open areas, suitable for different mountain biking experiences. Best mountain bike trails around La Horcajada The most popular mountain bike trail is El Tormes, la Garganta de los Caballeros y el Aravalle — Sierra de Gredos, a 19.0 miles (30.6 km) trail that takes 2 hours 41 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers views of the Sierra de Gredos mountains. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is Valle del Aravalle y Umbrías — circular desde El Barco de Ávila, a difficult 22.8 miles (36.8 km) path. This challenging route features significant elevation changes through the Aravalle Valley. Local mountain bikers also love the Corneja Valley Trail, a 17.3 miles (27.9 km) trail leading through the Corneja Valley, often completed in about 2 hours 33 minutes. Mountain biking around La Horcajada is defined by river valleys, rolling hills, and the presence of the Sierra de Gredos. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate paths to more challenging climbs. The routes in La Horcajada are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 150 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore La Horcajada's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many mountain bike trails are there in La Horcajada?
A: There are over 40 mountain bike trails around La Horcajada, offering a diverse range of experiences. These include 5 easy routes, 29 moderate routes, and 9 challenging trails for experienced riders.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on mountain bike trails in La Horcajada?
A: The terrain around La Horcajada is diverse, featuring river valleys, rolling hills, and the foothills of the Sierra de Gredos. You'll encounter varied elevation gains, with some routes climbing over 1,000 feet, providing both exhilarating rides and picturesque views.

Q: Are there any easy mountain bike trails suitable for beginners in La Horcajada?
A: Yes, La Horcajada offers 5 easy mountain bike trails perfect for beginners or those looking for a more relaxed ride. These routes generally have less demanding elevation changes and technical sections.

Q: What are the most challenging mountain bike trails in La Horcajada?
A: For experienced riders seeking a challenge, there are 9 difficult mountain bike trails. One such route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1084864592/valle-del-aravalle-y-umbrias-circular-desde-el-barco-de-avila" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valle del Aravalle y Umbrías — circular desde El Barco de Ávila&lt;/a&gt;, which features significant elevation changes over its 36.8 km length. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31873809" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Navalmahillo loop from La Aldehuela&lt;/a&gt;, covering nearly 60 km with over 1,600 meters of ascent.

Q: What do other mountain bikers enjoy most about the trails in La Horcajada?
A: The mountain bike trails in La Horcajada are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.9 stars from more than 10 reviews. Riders often praise the varied terrain, the scenic views of the Sierra de Gredos, and the options available for different skill levels.

Q: Are there any circular mountain bike routes in La Horcajada?
A: Yes, many routes in La Horcajada are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. An example is the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1084864592/valle-del-aravalle-y-umbrias-circular-desde-el-barco-de-avila" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valle del Aravalle y Umbrías — circular desde El Barco de Ávila&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a full loop experience through the Aravalle Valley.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the mountain bike trails?
A: The trails often pass through scenic river valleys and offer views of the Sierra de Gredos mountains. You can also explore historical sites like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3287205" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valdecorneja Castle&lt;/a&gt;, or visit the charming settlement of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4516184" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Barco de Ávila&lt;/a&gt;, which is a common starting point for some routes.

Q: Are there any specific trails that offer views of the Sierra de Gredos?
A: Yes, several trails provide excellent views of the Sierra de Gredos. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1379829215/el-tormes-la-garganta-de-los-caballeros-y-el-aravalle-sierra-de-gredos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Tormes, la Garganta de los Caballeros y el Aravalle — Sierra de Gredos&lt;/a&gt; route is specifically known for its mountain vistas.

Q: What is the best time of year for mountain biking in La Horcajada?
A: While specific seasonal advice isn't detailed, mountain biking in Spain is generally pleasant in spring and autumn when temperatures are milder. Summer can be hot, especially in inland areas, while winter might bring colder conditions at higher elevations. Checking local weather forecasts before your ride is always recommended.

Q: Are there any trails that follow river valleys?
A: Yes, the region is characterized by its river valleys. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1087818050/corneja-valley-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Corneja Valley Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, leading you through the picturesque Corneja Valley. The Aravalle Valley also features prominently in routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1379153081/aravalle-valley-and-umbrias-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aravalle Valley and Umbrías Loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Is there parking available for mountain bikers in La Horcajada?
A: While specific parking locations are not detailed, La Horcajada and nearby towns like El Barco de Ávila, which serve as starting points for many routes, typically offer parking facilities for visitors. It's advisable to check local information for specific parking areas near your chosen trailhead.

Q: Can I find mountain bike trails that are less crowded in La Horcajada?
A: La Horcajada offers a network of trails that cater to various preferences. While some routes are more popular, the region's extensive natural environment means you can often find quieter paths, especially during off-peak times or by exploring some of the less-trafficked moderate and difficult routes.
tips:
Old bridge, currently misnamed La Fonseca, its real historical name is La Ponseca. It belongs to the municipality of La Horcajada, which has also recently been rehabilitated because its rock was detaching and its stability was beginning to be endangered.
It crosses the river Corneja and as a curiosity, Emperor Carlos V passed over this bridge on his retirement journey to the Yuste monastery.
At this point I come across the parish church of the town of La Horcajada. I took advantage of my visit to this beautiful town to fill up the bottle at the fountain that we will find in the center.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this mountain bike route?
faqs.answer: The route offers a diverse range of terrain. You'll encounter varied sections with climbs, 'flowy' singletrack, and some rocky areas, particularly around La Aldehuela. Near Carrascalejo, expect technical sections with loose white sand and 'canchos' (rocky patches) that require careful handling. The area around the Ponseca Bridge can also feature demanding climbs and may become muddy after rain, adding to the technical challenge.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: This route combines natural beauty with historical significance. You'll pass by the historic Ponseca Bridge, a Roman bridge that adds a touch of ancient history. The route also goes near the Church of Our Lady of the Assumption. Beyond these highlights, you'll experience the unique natural settings of Las Hurdes, enjoyable views around the Proserpina reservoir near Carrascalejo, and the geologically rich Montes Obarenes with impressive limestone formations and diverse flora.

faqs.question: Is the route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Given the varied terrain, including technical sections with loose sand and rocks, demanding climbs, and potentially muddy conditions, this route is rated as moderate. While some parts are accessible, the more technical sections require some mountain biking skill. Beginners might find certain segments challenging.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to ride this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to ride this route is generally during spring or autumn. These seasons offer more pleasant temperatures and typically avoid the intense heat of summer or the potentially muddy and cold conditions of winter, especially in areas like the Montes Obarenes near the Ponseca Bridge.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail?
faqs.answer: As this is a composite route connecting several locations, parking options will vary. You can typically find parking in or near the villages of La Aldehuela, Carrascalejo, and Hoyorredondo. For the Ponseca Bridge section, look for parking near Foncea or other access points to the Montes Obarenes.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the trail?
faqs.answer: Generally, dogs are welcome on mountain biking trails in these rural areas, but it's always best to keep them on a leash, especially when passing through villages, near livestock, or in areas with wildlife. Be mindful of other trail users and ensure you clean up after your pet.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or is there an entrance fee for this route?
faqs.answer: No specific permits or entrance fees are generally required to ride this route. It traverses public and rural paths. However, always respect private property and local regulations, especially if you deviate from the main path.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route incorporates sections of other paths. Approximately 12% of the route follows the "Ruta del Rio Corneja y Hocino," and about 2% is part of the "Puente de la Ponseca" trail. The Carrascalejo section also touches upon historical routes like the Camino de Santiago and Vía de la Plata.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife can I expect to see?
faqs.answer: The diverse landscapes along the route offer opportunities for wildlife spotting. The Montes Obarenes area, particularly near the Ponseca Bridge, is a Special Protection Area for Birds (ZEPA), known for numerous raptor nests in its rocky cliffs. You might also encounter various other birds and small mammals typical of rural Spanish landscapes.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay near the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the villages along or near the route, such as La Aldehuela, Carrascalejo, and Hoyorredondo, offer options for refreshments and accommodation. You can find local restaurants, cafes, and rural guesthouses to refuel and rest after your ride.

faqs.question: What are the winter conditions like for mountain biking on this route?
faqs.answer: Winter conditions can vary. While some sections might remain rideable, areas at higher elevations or those prone to moisture, especially near the Ponseca Bridge in the Montes Obarenes, can become muddy, icy, or even snow-covered. Always check the local weather forecast before heading out in winter and be prepared for challenging conditions.

waypoints.name: Church of Our Lady of the Assumption
